Concentration creates multiple firing opportunities when the target passes through the ambush area. The answer highlights intelligence, chokepoints, and patience rather than an open-ocean chase.
segments.question: How could the Royal Navy use only submarines to sink an Imperial Japanese Navy carrier?
segments.answerShort: Concentrate T-class boats near a port or channel where the carrier's movement is predictable and ambush it there.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke recommends multiple T-class submarines positioned beside a port or constrained channel.

segments.title: An Italian special attack on New York Harbor
segments.summary: A successful Italian special-forces attack on New York Harbor would transform American coastal-defense priorities.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that a conspicuous harbor penetration would force the US Navy to rethink local defense against small, short-range attackers. The US Coast Guard could consequently receive a stronger role and more suitable coastal craft.
segments.question: How would a successful Italian attack on New York Harbor in 1943 change US naval thinking?
segments.answerShort: It would drive a major reassessment of coastal defense and likely strengthen Coast Guard short-range forces.

evidence.note: The response predicts changed US coastal-defense policy.

segments.title: Why Britain canceled later Monmouth and Topaze cruisers
segments.summary: The final Monmouth and additional Topaze cruisers lost priority when the Admiralty found more useful demands for the money.
segments.body: The answer is a procurement judgment rather than a claim that the existing ships were worthless. Changing priorities made additional members of the classes less attractive than competing programs.
segments.question: Why were the last Monmouth-class armored cruiser and four Topaze-class protected cruisers canceled?
segments.answerShort: They were no longer the best use of available funds compared with newer priorities.

evidence.note: The response says other uses for the money were judged more valuable.

segments.title: HMS Resolution as a forgotten battleship
segments.summary: HMS Resolution is proposed as a more thoroughly forgotten World War II battleship than USS Indiana despite the name's long Royal Navy history.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares how individual ships disappear even when their sisters remain familiar. Revenge, Royal Sovereign, Royal Oak, and Ramillies all have well-known stories, while students often cannot name Resolution as the fifth R-class battleship. That obscurity is striking because Resolution was a traditional Royal Navy name with numerous battle honors stretching from the age of sail to Norway. The segment invites viewers to separate class recognition from the historical memory of each hull.
segments.question: Which World War II battleship is most forgotten?
segments.answerShort: HMS Resolution is a strong candidate because even people familiar with the R class often overlook her despite the name's distinguished history.

evidence.note: The response contrasts Resolution with better-known sisters and lists the name's extensive battle honors.

segments.title: Why Fisher disposed of third-class torpedo cruisers
segments.summary: Fisher removed nine third-class torpedo cruisers because he considered other ships more useful and wanted the savings.
segments.body: The answer captures Fisher's readiness to prune marginal types from the fleet. Scrapping was part of redirecting money and manpower toward ships he believed offered greater operational value.
segments.question: Why did Admiral Fisher scrap an entire flotilla of third-class torpedo cruisers?
segments.answerShort: He thought better ships were available and wanted to save the resources tied up in obsolete vessels.

evidence.note: The answer cites better alternatives and financial savings.

segments.title: The limits of rebuilding Napoleon's navy
segments.summary: The French fleet needed more ships, more training, and far more time at sea, but those reforms still had to operate through the existing French system.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke's priorities begin with practical sea experience rather than a single new design or tactical trick. Crews and officers need sustained training together, supported by a larger usable fleet. Those changes take time and require institutional backing that Napoleon's naval administration struggled to provide. The answer therefore remains pessimistic about transforming the fleet enough before 1805 even with better advice.
segments.question: What could an adviser to Napoleon do from 1801 to improve the French navy before Trafalgar?
segments.answerShort: Increase sea time, training, and ship numbers, though the French system makes success before 1805 unlikely.

evidence.note: The response calls for more time at sea, training, and ships while doubting the institutional ability to deliver them.

segments.title: Expanding British shipyards before World War II
segments.summary: Maintaining and expanding shipyards during the 1930s would let Britain exploit more of its maritime industrial potential and enter the war with more ships.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke gives the direct industrial answer: capacity that survives the interwar contraction can build wartime hulls sooner. More slips, workers, and supporting plant reduce the delay between rearmament decisions and ships entering service. The benefit is broader than any single class because every program competes for the same yards and skills. The segment captures why industrial readiness is itself part of fleet strength.
segments.question: How would World War II change if Britain expanded shipyards during the 1930s instead of closing them?
segments.answerShort: Britain would preserve more maritime industrial capacity and build substantially more ships when rearmament began.

evidence.note: The response links expanded yards directly to greater wartime ship production.

segments.title: What might have followed the Lion class
segments.summary: The Admiralty expected another generation after Lion but deliberately waited for experience and foreign developments before fixing a design.
segments.body: The absence of a completed successor plan does not mean Britain intended to stop building battleships. The Admiralty wanted to see how the Lion design and international programs developed before committing to the next class. Sixteen-inch guns remained the likely starting point. Discovery of the Yamato class or other heavily armed foreign ships could have pushed the follow-on toward 18-inch weapons.
segments.question: Did the Royal Navy have plans for a battleship class after Lion before World War II?
segments.answerShort: It expected a successor but had not fixed the design, leaving room for either another 16-inch ship or an 18-inch response to foreign developments.

evidence.note: The response says future classes were expected but deferred pending experience and possible knowledge of Yamato.

segments.title: Preparing Russia's Far Eastern fleet in 1900
segments.summary: Russia needed stronger port defenses, deeper supply stocks, better garrisons, and ships designed for reliable long-range movement before war with Japan.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke starts with the bases because a fleet cannot operate if its anchorage and communications collapse. Port Arthur and other key sites need stronger fortifications, prepared garrisons, and supplies stored before a blockade. The fleet also requires ships able to move over long distances without depending on fragile local arrangements. The advice links tactical survival at the port to the broader logistics of reinforcing and sustaining the Far Eastern force.
segments.question: What ships and infrastructure should a Russian naval adviser improve in 1900 before war with Japan?
segments.answerShort: Strengthen port defenses and garrisons, stockpile supplies, and acquire more capable long-range ships.

evidence.note: The response calls for port defenses, supplied garrisons, and better long-range ships.

segments.title: Would the Royal Navy coin 'heavy cruiser' without treaties?
segments.summary: Without treaty definitions, the Royal Navy would have little reason to create the specific category 'heavy cruiser.'
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the label as a legal and administrative product rather than a natural technical category. Without treaty gun-caliber boundaries, British classification would likely continue along different cruiser lines.
segments.question: Would the Royal Navy have coined the term 'heavy cruiser' without naval treaties?
segments.answerShort: Probably not, because treaty rules created the need for that distinct classification.

evidence.note: The response connects the term directly to treaty definitions.

segments.title: Shetland as a central North Sea base
segments.summary: Moving British Shetland into the central North Sea would create a valuable but heavily contested air and naval base that changes both world wars.
segments.body: The islands would sit astride German and British movements rather than on the edge of the theater. Britain would build major airfields there, extending fighter coverage and reconnaissance across the North Sea. Those aircraft could influence the Norwegian campaign and intercept operations that historically crossed a wider gap. The same value would make the islands a constant objective for German attack and a demanding position to defend.
segments.question: What changes if British Shetland lies in the middle of the North Sea?
segments.answerShort: It becomes a powerful but fiercely contested air and naval base, altering North Sea operations and the Norwegian campaign.

evidence.note: The response describes contested geography, a large air base, fighter coverage, and effects on Norway.

segments.title: A Royal Navy with eighty modern cruisers
segments.summary: Roughly forty County-class and forty light cruisers would double British cruiser strength and smother German surface-raiding operations.
segments.body: The most immediate difference is density: hunting groups can cover more routes and mass more ships at contact. Harwood might meet Admiral Graf Spee with six cruisers rather than three at the River Plate. Similar reinforcements would follow German raiders across the world's trade lanes. Dr. Clarke therefore expects commerce raiding to become a much shorter and more dangerous career for German surface ships.
segments.question: How quickly would German commerce raiding fail if Britain entered World War II with about forty County-class and forty light cruisers?
segments.answerShort: Very quickly; British hunting groups would be roughly twice as strong and could overwhelm raiders such as Admiral Graf Spee.

evidence.note: The answer doubles Harwood's cruiser group and predicts Graf Spee cannot escape.

segments.title: Britain enters the world wars with domestic oil
segments.summary: Large domestic oil production would make Britain far more secure by reducing the fuel imports and shipping exposure that constrained both world wars.
segments.body: The fleet, air force, industry, and civilian economy all depended on imported fuel moving through threatened sea lanes. A major domestic source removes part of that burden from tankers, escorts, and foreign exchange. Britain can support its armed forces with less vulnerability to blockade and shipping loss. Dr. Clarke therefore expects the effect to be dramatic in both world wars rather than confined to naval operations.
segments.question: How would the world wars change if Britain were a major oil power?
segments.answerShort: Britain would be far more secure and able to fuel its war effort without relying so heavily on vulnerable imports.

evidence.note: The response says domestic fuel would make Britain very happy and dramatically change both wars.

segments.title: British choices for a larger destroyer gun
segments.summary: For a larger interwar destroyer, British designers considered a band from the familiar 4.7-inch gun through 5- and 5.5-inch weapons.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke does not identify one inevitable caliber because the choice follows the destroyer's mission and size. The established 4.7-inch weapon offered a practical baseline. Five-inch and 5.5-inch alternatives promised heavier shells if the hull could accept their mountings and handling burden. The watch point helps place British destroyer armament inside the wider fleet hierarchy of 6-inch cruisers and larger capital-ship guns.
segments.question: What gun caliber would Britain consider for a larger non-treaty destroyer?
segments.answerShort: Probably 4.7, 5, or 5.5 inches, depending on the hull and role.

evidence.note: The response identifies the 4.7-, 5-, and 5.5-inch options.

segments.title: Could the Union Navy challenge HMS Warrior?
segments.summary: No Union ship could meet HMS Warrior on the open sea; the only plausible answer was an ambush in confined coastal waters that the Royal Navy would avoid.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke gives Warrior decisive advantages in speed, endurance, seaworthiness, and combat power away from shore. American monitors and other ironclads become dangerous only if the British ship enters narrow water where several defenders can concentrate at close range. The Royal Navy has no reason to accept that trap and can use smaller vessels for the confined approach. The comparison shows how a ship can dominate one environment while remaining vulnerable in another it is free to avoid.
segments.question: Did the Union Navy have anything that could realistically challenge HMS Warrior during the American Civil War?
segments.answerShort: Not on the open sea; only a concentrated ambush in confined waters offered a plausible chance, and Warrior need not enter them.

evidence.note: The response denies an open-sea match and confines the Union chance to a close-water ambush.

segments.title: Building Omaha-class cruisers throughout the 1910s
segments.summary: A steady Omaha-class program would have served the United States better than allowing its cruiser force to stagnate through much of the 1910s.
segments.body: The answer is brief but rests on a clear force-structure problem. The US Navy needed modern scouts and overseas cruisers rather than a long gap in construction. Repeated Omaha-type orders would preserve design experience and produce useful hulls before postwar treaty programs. Even if later ships evolved, continuity would leave the navy better prepared than sporadic procurement.
segments.question: Would the United States have been better off building Omaha-class cruisers steadily through the 1910s?
segments.answerShort: Yes; continuous cruiser construction would have been better than the historical gap.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke gives an unqualified yes to sustained Omaha-class construction.

segments.title: What British gun choices reveal about postwar ambition
segments.summary: Planned gun calibers show a Royal Navy designing distinct ship types for global duties and possible future opponents.
segments.body: The answer reads weapon choices as evidence of intended missions. Reliable six-inch light cruisers support worldwide work, larger cruiser guns answer harder opponents, and heavy capital-ship weapons preserve superiority in the most demanding fleet engagements.
segments.question: What do planned British battleship, battlecruiser, and cruiser gun calibers reveal about postwar naval ambitions?
segments.answerShort: They show a globally oriented fleet matching each ship category to different opponents and operational demands.

evidence.note: The response links gun tiers to global tasks and expected opponents.

segments.title: An effective self-propelled torpedo before 1857
segments.summary: An earlier practical torpedo would accelerate torpedo craft and the destroyers built to counter them, pushing both types toward larger designs sooner.
segments.body: A viable weapon changes naval construction only when ships can carry and employ it in useful numbers. Dr. Clarke expects torpedo boats to appear earlier and create an earlier demand for torpedo-boat destroyers. As the attack threat grows, the countering ships also gain size, endurance, and armament. The exchange captures the paired evolution of an offensive technology and the platform created to defeat it.
segments.question: How would a viable self-propelled torpedo before 1857 change naval construction?
segments.answerShort: Torpedo craft and the destroyers built to counter them would appear and grow larger much earlier.

evidence.note: The answer predicts earlier torpedo boats and earlier, larger destroyers.

segments.title: Who could lead the US Navy instead of King
segments.summary: James O. Richardson is identified as the strongest alternative to Ernest King, although his return would renew a serious conflict with Roosevelt.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke selects Richardson on professional ability rather than political ease. Richardson had the experience and judgment for senior wartime command, and without King his path to the top becomes much clearer. His earlier opposition to Roosevelt's basing decisions, however, had already produced a rupture with the president. Appointing him would therefore trade King's difficult personality for a different but equally consequential civil-military argument.
segments.question: Who could lead the US Navy before and during World War II if Ernest King were unavailable?
segments.answerShort: James O. Richardson was the most capable alternative, though Roosevelt would face a major argument with him.

evidence.note: The response names Richardson and notes the likely conflict with Roosevelt.

segments.title: Planning for nuclear carrier dismantling
segments.summary: A nuclear-carrier program should include a dismantling route from the beginning, though the final facility might be located elsewhere in a large empire.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke says designers would aim to provide an end-of-life solution just as the US Navy intended to do. Building reactors into large ships creates a decades-later requirement for specialized yards, waste handling, and trained personnel. A British imperial program might place that work in India or another suitable industrial location rather than Britain itself. The answer highlights decommissioning as part of the full lifecycle cost of nuclear naval power.
segments.question: Would a nuclear-powered British Empire have facilities to dismantle nuclear aircraft carriers at the end of their lives?
segments.answerShort: It should plan for them, though the specialized dismantling site might be located in India or another imperial industrial center.

evidence.note: The response expects planned disposal capacity and suggests it might be located in India.

segments.title: The case for returning naval aviation to the Admiralty
segments.summary: RAF planning that treated carrier aircraft as reinforcements for land bases provides the strongest evidence that the Fleet Air Arm and maritime air power needed naval control.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke would build the case from operational plans rather than abstract service preference. RAF documents for a Far Eastern war proposed loading aircraft onto carriers, sending them east, and then removing the aircraft for land operations. That approach left the fleet without the air group the carriers existed to operate. Showing the plan's contradiction would make the Admiralty argument that maritime aviation required different priorities difficult to answer.
segments.question: What incontrovertible case could the Admiralty make in 1930 to recover the Fleet Air Arm and Coastal Command from the RAF?
segments.answerShort: Use RAF plans that stripped deployed carriers of their aircraft to prove maritime aviation was not receiving fleet-centered priorities.

evidence.note: The answer cites Far Eastern plans to move carrier aircraft ashore and leave the fleet without them.

segments.title: Fisher's least successful wartime ships
segments.summary: The Courageous class and HMS Furious are identified as Fisher's greatest wartime design failures, while HMS Captain belongs to a different peacetime context.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke separates spectacularly odd ships from projects created under different political and experimental conditions. The large light cruisers consumed wartime resources for roles that never justified their extreme compromises. Furious magnified the problem with an armament and layout that quickly demanded conversion. Captain remains a notorious failure, but she was a peacetime test rather than the same kind of wartime procurement decision.
segments.question: What was the biggest wartime turkey associated with Jacky Fisher?
segments.answerShort: The Courageous class, including Furious, because their wartime design compromises produced little value.

evidence.note: The response names the Courageous class and Furious while distinguishing the peacetime HMS Captain.

segments.title: What mattered most in the loss of USS Hornet
segments.summary: Hornet's hull was the most strategically important loss at that moment, while experienced crew mattered far more than the aircraft that American production could replace quickly.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke normally puts human loss first, but late 1942 left the United States with so few carriers that the ship itself had exceptional strategic value. The deaths and loss of trained crew still damaged the navy because experience could not be manufactured immediately. By contrast, the lost aircraft represented only a small fraction of daily American production. The comparison shows how the value of hulls, people, and machines changes with scarcity at a particular moment in a campaign.
segments.question: How should the loss of USS Hornet be divided between the carrier, her crew, and her aircraft?
segments.answerShort: The scarce carrier was the most important strategic loss, experienced crew came next, and the aircraft were comparatively easy to replace.

evidence.note: The response ranks the carrier, experienced crew, and rapidly replaceable aircraft by wartime scarcity.

segments.title: A twelve-gun Hawkins-class cruiser
segments.summary: Britain had the guns and twin-turret experience to build Hawkins-class ships with twelve 7.5-inch guns, though the layout would require a much more elaborate arrangement.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sees no fundamental technical barrier if the Admiralty truly wanted the design. Britain possessed both 7.5-inch weapons and workable twin-mount concepts. A twelve-gun layout could use pairs of turrets forward and aft with additional wing positions, while an even larger battery would multiply those compromises. The answer separates feasibility from desirability: a ship can be drawn and built without being the best use of displacement.
segments.question: Could Britain build Hawkins-class cruisers with twelve 7.5-inch guns in six twin turrets?
segments.answerShort: Yes; the guns and turret designs existed, although arranging six mounts would complicate the ship.

evidence.note: The response says the design was technically possible and sketches forward, aft, and wing-turret arrangements.

segments.title: Britain's prewar cruiser shortage
segments.summary: The Royal Navy needed more heavy cruisers before World War II, but its deeper problem was a shortage of cruisers of every kind.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke answers yes while widening the frame beyond one subtype. Britain's worldwide commitments created a demand for cruiser hulls that budgets and construction capacity struggled to satisfy across both heavy and light categories.
segments.question: Should the Royal Navy have built more heavy cruisers before World War II?
segments.answerShort: Yes, but it needed more cruisers overall and was already building as many as constraints allowed.

evidence.note: The response broadens the issue to total cruiser numbers.

segments.title: Why Germany had no answer to the Hawkins class
segments.summary: Germany produced no meaningful answer to the Hawkins class because wartime priorities and the postwar settlement left neither the construction capacity nor program for one.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke rejects the assumption that every foreign design automatically produces an opposing class. Hawkins emerged across the end of the First World War, when German yards were already failing to produce enough of their existing priorities. Defeat and postwar restrictions then removed the opportunity for a direct reply. The absence of a response therefore reflects industrial and political incapacity rather than German designers failing to notice the ship.
segments.question: How did Imperial Germany respond to the Hawkins-class cruiser?
segments.answerShort: It did not; Germany lacked the wartime capacity and postwar freedom to build a meaningful counterpart.

evidence.note: The response explains that German construction was already inadequate and postwar conditions prevented a reply.

segments.title: Why Britain could not readily adopt the F-14A
segments.summary: Buying the F-14A in the 1970s makes little sense without a carrier such as CVA-01 capable of operating it.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke makes the flight deck the decisive constraint. The aircraft cannot be assessed independently of the catapults, arresting gear, deck space, maintenance facilities, and carrier program required to deploy it at sea.
segments.question: Should the Royal Navy or RAF have bought the F-14A in the 1970s?
segments.answerShort: Not without CVA-01 or another carrier large enough to operate and support the aircraft.

evidence.note: The answer identifies the absence of a suitable carrier as decisive.

segments.title: Why triple 6-inch turrets would dominate treaty cruisers
segments.summary: If First World War light cruisers already normalized twin 6-inch mounts, treaty-era British designs would probably adopt triples.
segments.body: The answer emphasizes the efficient increase in barrels per unit of protected mounting weight. Once multiple-gun turrets are mature, a triple offers an attractive way to raise firepower within tight displacement limits.
segments.question: Would British treaty cruisers use triple 6-inch turrets if earlier light cruisers already used twins?
segments.answerShort: Very likely, because triples add firepower efficiently within treaty tonnage limits.

evidence.note: The response stresses the weight and tonnage efficiency of triple mounts.

segments.title: Which admiral most shaped World War II
segments.summary: Yamamoto, Cunningham, Nimitz, and Noble each shaped a different decisive part of the naval war, making the answer depend on how strategic impact is measured.
segments.body: Yamamoto helped set Japan and the United States on the path to the Pacific War through his operational plans. Cunningham influenced Britain's Mediterranean and global naval effort, while Nimitz directed the American Pacific campaign. Percy Noble helped establish the Battle of the Atlantic command system and the Anglo-American relationship later carried forward by Horton. Dr. Clarke concludes that ranking them requires explicit criteria and may be better suited to a comparative book than a single name.
segments.question: Which admiral made the biggest impact during World War II?
segments.answerShort: There is no single easy winner: Yamamoto, Cunningham, Nimitz, and Noble each shaped a different decisive theater or coalition system.

evidence.note: The response compares Yamamoto, Cunningham, Nimitz, and Noble and declines a simple ranking.

segments.title: The most common practical carrier type
segments.summary: The most common useful carrier is a light or medium ship able to perform many fleet-carrier tasks without the cost and size of a full fleet carrier.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke avoids forcing every aviation ship into one rigid label because national classifications vary. The recurring practical type is large enough to support meaningful fixed-wing or rotary-wing operations yet small enough for more navies to afford. Invincible-class ships illustrate how such carriers can undertake fleet work under pressure without matching a supercarrier in air-group size. The answer explains why broadly capable light carriers appear repeatedly across different fleets.
segments.question: What is the most common type of aircraft carrier built across navies?
segments.answerShort: A light or medium carrier able to approach fleet-carrier work without the full size and expense of a fleet carrier.

evidence.note: The response describes a broadly capable carrier below full fleet-carrier size and cites Invincible.

segments.title: Early turreted secondary batteries
segments.summary: A successful turreted secondary battery on an early dreadnought would spread quickly because it improves arcs, permits more effective weapons, and supports later anti-aircraft development.
segments.body: Casemate guns are constrained by hull openings, sea conditions, and limited arcs. Turrets let a secondary battery train more freely and concentrate fire where it is needed. They also make it easier to adopt larger or dual-purpose weapons as aircraft become a threat. Dr. Clarke therefore expects other navies to copy the arrangement rapidly once a practical example demonstrates the advantages.
segments.question: How quickly would turreted secondary batteries catch on if Britain fitted them to an early dreadnought?
segments.answerShort: Very quickly, because better arcs and more effective guns would also improve the path toward anti-aircraft defense.

evidence.note: The response emphasizes training arcs, weapon effectiveness, and anti-aircraft utility.

segments.title: Swapping Beatty, Byng, and Collingwood
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke's preferred command exchange places Byng with the battlecruisers, Collingwood in Beatty's role, and Beatty in Byng's difficult historical position.
segments.body: The proposal tests how communication, staff quality, and temperament interact with command circumstances. Byng's historical retreat had a logical basis, and Dr. Clarke thinks he might manage a battlecruiser force more carefully and communicate better with superiors and subordinates. Collingwood then replaces Beatty, bringing proven fleet-command qualities to the role. Beatty receives Byng's position, where his instinct to charge could expose the weaknesses that political mythology later obscured.
segments.question: If three admirals could exchange places, which swap would most improve naval history?
segments.answerShort: Put Byng with the battlecruisers, Collingwood in Beatty's place, and Beatty in Byng's historical command.

evidence.note: The response develops the Byng, Collingwood, and Beatty circular swap and explains their contrasting command styles.

segments.title: Why British medium AA guns resisted standardization
segments.summary: A vast inherited fleet and ammunition establishment made medium anti-aircraft standardization a lower British priority before the 1950s.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke points to the cost of supporting a large service inventory rather than simple indifference to commonality. Existing weapons, stocks, mountings, and ships created a powerful logistical reason to tolerate several calibers.
segments.question: Why was medium anti-aircraft gun standardization not a high British priority before the 1950s?
segments.answerShort: The navy had a vast existing force and supply system to support, making rapid standardization expensive and disruptive.

evidence.note: The response cites the scale of the existing navy and support burden.

segments.title: Nelson commanding the Grand Fleet
segments.summary: Nelson would probably retain the distant blockade in 1914 because Jellicoe's basic strategy was sound.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke separates command personality from strategic circumstance. Even an aggressive Nelson inherits geography, mines, submarines, and Britain's need to preserve the fleet, so he is unlikely to discard the distant blockade merely to seek a dramatic battle.
segments.question: What would Nelson do if placed in command of the Grand Fleet in World War I?
segments.answerShort: He would probably maintain the distant blockade because Jellicoe's strategic approach was already correct.

evidence.note: The response predicts Nelson would preserve Jellicoe's distant blockade.
